affordances.name: Measured vital-few effort allocation
affordances.mechanism: When outcomes are driven by a skewed contribution pattern, prove the uneven distribution, name the vital few and trivial many, redirect scarce time, resources, and analytical effort to the high-impact contributors, and set retest guards so 80/20 does not become unmeasured rhetoric or premature culling of future drivers.

use_when: A small subset of causes, customers, tasks, products, branches, or constraints appears to drive a disproportionate share of the outcome.
Time, attention, or resources are scarce enough that the answer must decide what not to work on, not merely what matters.
A complex problem tree has many branches and analysis will improve by cutting low-impact branches after likely impact is assessed.
The current distribution can be measured and revisited as the vital few shift over time.
do_not_use_when: The case has no measured or plausibly measurable skewed-return pattern and 80/20 language is only a convenience slogan.
The proposed pruning is based on ease, emotional comfort, or already-favored priorities instead of likely impact.
Emerging products, markets, ideas, or drivers are too early to measure fairly and current-state culling would likely discard future value.
The work requires root-cause diagnosis or human-context detail that would be hidden by treating a correlation as the cause.
case_evidence_needed: The outcome metric and candidate inputs, causes, customers, products, tasks, or branches being compared.
Evidence that contribution is highly uneven, including which subset appears to produce disproportionate output.
The scarce time, attention, budget, or analytical effort that must be redirected or withheld.
The low-impact branches, tasks, activities, or contributors proposed for minimization, streamlining, elimination, or deferral.
The emerging opportunities, future drivers, assumptions, or root-cause uncertainties that could invalidate the current vital-few map.

treatment_requirements.description: Before allowing Pareto to carry weight, show that the case has a skewed contribution pattern and that the selected vital few are derived from measured or explicitly reasoned likely impact rather than a fixed 80/20 slogan.
evidence_required: The relevant outcome or output being optimized.
The candidate causes, inputs, customers, products, tasks, constraints, or branches under comparison.
The data, estimate, or case reasoning showing a minority of contributors creates disproportionate output.
The reason the selected contributors are likely causal or diagnostically important, not merely correlated with the outcome.
good_output_shape: States the actual skew observed or hypothesized instead of treating 80/20 as a literal ratio.
Names the specific vital few and the evidence that they drive disproportionate impact.
Separates measured leverage from convenience, salience, or confirmation of already-favored priorities.
Says not to use Pareto when the case lacks a credible uneven-contribution pattern.

treatment_requirements.description: Translate the vital-few finding into an allocation decision by moving scarce resources and analytical effort toward the high-impact contributors while naming what low-impact work is minimized, streamlined, eliminated, or not analyzed further.
evidence_required: The scarce resources, attention, time, or analysis budget available.
The high-impact contributors that should receive more effort.
The low-impact activities, problem-tree branches, or data-gathering paths that do not contribute enough to justify more work.
The concrete reallocation, pruning, or don't-do choice that proves the recommendation changed effort allocation.
good_output_shape: Redirects resources and effort to the named key contributors.
Names the trivial many that will be minimized, streamlined, eliminated, deferred, or left unanalyzed.
Shows how pruning improves clarity or impact rather than merely shortening the work.
Keeps the output distinct from generic prioritization by tying every focus move to disproportionate contribution.

treatment_requirements.description: Treat the current vital few as temporary leverage points to monitor, then add review triggers or protected tests so emerging drivers, breakthrough ideas, and root-cause uncertainty are not discarded too early.
evidence_required: The emerging products, markets, ideas, or drivers that could become important despite current low contribution.
The assumptions embedded in the current vital-few map.
The indicators or threshold that would show the distribution has shifted.
The review cadence, scenario check, or small protected trial that keeps premature culling from becoming neglect.
good_output_shape: Defines when the vital-few allocation should be revisited.
Names what evidence would invalidate the current pruning decision.
Protects plausible future drivers from being eliminated solely because current metrics are small.
Keeps Pareto use paired with diagnosis when the apparent 20 percent may be a symptom rather than a cause.

diagnostic_questions: Which minority of inputs, causes, customers, tasks, products, branches, or constraints actually drives the disproportionate outcome?
What measured leverage shows that these are the vital few rather than the most familiar or politically convenient few?
What low-impact work, branch, customer segment, product, or activity will be minimized, streamlined, eliminated, or not analyzed further?
Are we trimming based on likely impact, or because some areas are easier or less emotionally taxing to ignore?
What assumptions are embedded in the current vital-few map, and what future evidence would invalidate them?
misuse_guards: Do not use Pareto as generic prioritization; require evidence of skewed contribution and a concrete effort-allocation change.
Do not treat 80/20 as a strict numerical law when the source frames it as a guideline whose percentage can vary.
Do not invoke vital-few language without measuring leverage or naming the contributors being deprioritized.
Do not prune emerging products, markets, or breakthrough ideas solely because current metrics make them look low-impact.
Do not confuse correlation with root cause; pair the vital-few map with diagnosis before acting on it.
Do not let simplification hide human context, implementation authority, or second-order system effects when those details control the outcome.

source_evidence.source_quote: a minority of inputs or causes generates a disproportionate majority of the outputs or outcomes.
it is **more of a guideline than a strict rule**, and the precise percentage can vary significantly.
It directs analytical effort toward the **most important factors** to maximize impact.
It is about recognizing that not all resources or efforts contribute equally to the final outcome.
cutting off the low-impact branches
identify the crucial **20%** of activities, customers, or products that deliver the maximum results, and then **redirect resources and efforts to maximize the impact of those key contributors**.
What is the low-impact **80%** that can be minimized, streamlined, or eliminated to gain greater clarity and freedom?

**Most useful when contribution is highly uneven:** The Pareto Principle is strongest when a small subset of causes, customers, tasks, or constraints clearly drives a disproportionate share of the result.
**Works when time or attention is scarce:** The model has high leverage when decision quality depends more on cutting low-yield effort than on squeezing completeness out of every branch.
**Best when prioritization can be revisited as the distribution shifts:** Pareto works best when the vital few are treated as current leverage points to monitor, not as eternal truths to defend.

A common anti-pattern is focusing **solely on the 20% of products that generate 80% of the revenue**, which can lead to **neglecting emerging products or markets that could be crucial for future growth**.
Prematurely trimming ideas based on early, low-impact metrics can lead to abandoning breakthrough concepts before they have a chance to prove their value.
The rule identifies correlations (20% input, 80% output), but it must be used alongside rigorous **diagnosis** to identify the root causes.
The model is distorted when teams invoke 80/20 rhetoric without actually measuring leverage, using it to justify ignoring inconvenient work or underinvesting in future drivers.

review_notes.normalization_note: Collapsed skewed-return detection, vital-few selection, pruning, effort reallocation, revisitable focus, and misuse guards into one affordance because the source presents them as one Pareto mechanism: measured uneven contribution should change where scarce effort goes.
dropped_material: Did not create a separate generic prioritization affordance; the source-backed contract requires skewed contribution and leverage evidence.
Did not create a standalone problem-tree pruning affordance; pruning is the analytical form of the same measured vital-few allocation mechanism.
Did not create a standalone root-cause or scenario-analysis affordance; the source uses these as guards against misusing Pareto, not as distinct Pareto runtime contracts.
Did not produce a third affordance, so the PR 9 structural differentiation test is not triggered.
open_questions: Reviewer should check the boundary with Batch 1 trade-offs and leverage-points plus the deferred prioritization model: Pareto should require skewed-return evidence, not merely scarce-resource sacrifice or generic ranking.
Reviewer should confirm whether the protected-trial language in the retest requirement is acceptable as normalization from the source's warnings about emerging markets and premature pruning.

absence_records.reason: The source supports prioritization only when contribution is highly uneven, leverage is measured, and effort actually shifts toward the vital few. A generic task-ranking affordance would erase the skewed-return and measurement guard that distinguish Pareto from the separate prioritization model.

source_evidence.source_quote: **Most useful when contribution is highly uneven:** The Pareto Principle is strongest when a small subset of causes, customers, tasks, or constraints clearly drives a disproportionate share of the result.
The model is distorted when teams invoke 80/20 rhetoric without actually measuring leverage, using it to justify ignoring inconvenient work or underinvesting in future drivers.

absence_records.reason: The source explicitly says the ratio is a guideline rather than a strict rule, so runtime use should not require or assert an exact 80 percent to 20 percent distribution before the model can apply.

source_evidence.source_quote: While the ratio is often cited as 80/20, it is **more of a guideline than a strict rule**, and the precise percentage can vary significantly.

absence_records.reason: The source warns that Pareto correlations need rigorous diagnosis, but it does not make root-cause analysis a distinct Pareto affordance. Diagnosis belongs here as a misuse guard and treatment check, while a fuller root-cause contract belongs to the root-cause-analysis model.

source_evidence.source_quote: The rule identifies correlations (20% input, 80% output), but it must be used alongside rigorous **diagnosis** to identify the root causes.
a minority of inputs or causes generates a disproportionate majority of the outputs or outcomes.
identify the crucial **20%** of activities, customers, or products that deliver the maximum results, and then **redirect resources and efforts to maximize the impact of those key contributors**.
**Works when time or attention is scarce:** The model has high leverage when decision quality depends more on cutting low-yield effort than on squeezing completeness out of every branch.
**Best when prioritization can be revisited as the distribution shifts:** Pareto works best when the vital few are treated as current leverage points to monitor, not as eternal truths to defend.
The model is distorted when teams invoke 80/20 rhetoric without actually measuring leverage, using it to justify ignoring inconvenient work or underinvesting in future drivers.

review_notes.normalization_note: Read the extraction contract, schema, PR 9 Batch 2 selection note, full Pareto_Principle_rag.md source, Wave 1 activation curation, Wave 2 intervention semantics, and Wave 3 relation semantics. Extracted one high-confidence affordance that preserves Pareto as measured skewed-return effort allocation and vital-few pruning, not generic prioritization.
dropped_material: Did not split customer strategy, policy trade-off, healthcare cost, deep work, Bloom's Taxonomy, bounded rationality, or communication examples into separate affordances because they illustrate the same uneven-contribution mechanism in different domains.

Did not promote relation-layer allies or tensions into separate runtime contracts; systems-thinking, circle-of-control, and confirmation-bias material is reflected only as boundary and misuse-guard context.

Did not produce a third affordance; the source does not support three structurally distinct Pareto runtime contracts without drifting into prioritization, leverage-points, root-cause-analysis, experimentation, or systems-thinking.
open_questions: Reviewer should confirm that one affordance is intentionally sparse enough for the resource-allocation risk: the affordance activates only when skewed-return evidence changes effort allocation.
Reviewer should verify whether the root-cause diagnosis guard is sufficient or should be reduced further to avoid overlap with root-cause-analysis.
